evals.prompt: My reviewer left 12 comments on my PR. Three are bugs they caught (real ones), four are nits about naming, three are suggestions for refactors that would expand the PR scope, and two I think are wrong. How do I respond?
evals.expected_output: Triage each independently. The three bugs: fix them, respond 'Good catch, fixed in <commit>.' on each. The four nits: most likely fix them quickly unless they actively make the code worse; if they have a common theme, suggest tightening the linter for the next PR. The three out-of-scope refactors: acknowledge each is a valid suggestion, decline as out-of-scope for this PR, file follow-up issues and link from the PR. The two you think are wrong: push back with the specific technical argument, not 'I disagree'. Every comment gets a response of some kind. Re-request review only after everything is addressed or explicitly in discussion.
assertions: Recommends fixing the three bugs immediately
Recommends fixing most nits rather than arguing
Suggests tightening the linter if nits have a common theme
Recommends declining out-of-scope refactors with follow-up issues
Recommends pushing back on the two disagreed comments with technical reasoning
Reinforces that every comment gets a response (even nits)
Recommends re-requesting only after all comments addressed or explicitly in discussion

evals.prompt: My reviewer is asking me to refactor a helper to use a pattern from another codebase. I don't think it fits here. How do I push back?
evals.expected_output: Push back specifically, not generally. State the technical reason the pattern doesn't fit (e.g., 'this codebase uses pattern X for this case, see <example>'). Ask the reviewer to elaborate on the benefit they see in the suggested pattern; sometimes the clarification reveals you misread the suggestion or they misread the code. If the disagreement is genuine and unresolved, propose a third reviewer or area owner as a tiebreaker. Do NOT capitulate just to ship; do NOT escalate before engaging the technical argument directly with the reviewer.
assertions: Recommends pushing back with the specific technical reason
Recommends asking the reviewer to elaborate on their reasoning
References this codebase's existing conventions
Recommends tiebreaker (third reviewer) only if direct engagement fails
Does not recommend capitulation just to ship
Does not recommend escalating to management as a first move
Frames as discussion to resolve, not battle to win

evals.prompt: A reviewer wrote 'this could be cleaner' on a function I wrote. I'm not sure if they want me to refactor or if it's a nit. What do I do?
evals.expected_output: Ask for clarification before responding. The comment is ambiguous between nit, suggestion, and blocker. A clean clarification: 'I want to make sure I understand — are you suggesting a specific change, or is this a general impression? If specific, what shape would you want it to take?' Asking is cheaper than guessing and refactoring the wrong thing. Do NOT just refactor based on assumption; do NOT argue against a position the reviewer hasn't actually stated.
assertions: Recommends asking for clarification before responding
Identifies the comment as ambiguous between nit, suggestion, and blocker
Provides a specific clarifying question shape
Does not recommend refactoring on assumption
Does not recommend arguing against an unstated position
Frames asking as faster than guessing wrong

evals.prompt: My reviewer wants me to add tests for an edge case I think is genuinely impossible to hit. The code is structured so that case never reaches this function. Should I just add the test?
evals.expected_output: Push back with the structural argument. State specifically why the case can't reach the function (the caller, the type system, the upstream validation). Offer the structural evidence: 'the caller at <line> filters out X before calling this function, so this case can never reach here'. If the structural evidence is real, the test would be testing a contradiction. If the reviewer is still concerned, suggest a comment in the code documenting the invariant, or a defensive assert that fails loudly if the impossible case ever occurs (which doubles as a test surface). Do NOT add a test that asserts a tautology; do NOT just capitulate without surfacing the structural argument.
assertions: Recommends pushing back with the structural argument
Provides specific evidence (caller, type system, upstream validation)
Suggests a code comment or defensive assert as an alternative to a tautological test
Does not recommend adding a test that asserts a contradiction
Does not recommend capitulating without engaging the technical argument
Allows the reviewer's concern to be addressed in a different way

evals.prompt: I addressed 8 of 10 review comments. The other 2 I disagreed with and replied with my reasoning. Should I re-request review or wait for them to respond?
evals.expected_output: Re-request review now. The PR is back in the reviewer's court: 8 things to verify and 2 discussion items waiting for them. The signal to the reviewer is 'I've done my part; please look'. Note in the re-request message what state things are in ('addressed 8 of 10; 2 open discussion items at <links>'). Do NOT wait silently — silence after responding looks like inactivity. Do NOT re-request before addressing everything; the half-done state was the wrong signal. Now is the right time.
assertions: Recommends re-requesting review now
Notes the PR is back in the reviewer's court
Recommends a brief summary of state in the re-request message
Identifies silence after responding as the wrong signal
Identifies premature re-request as a different anti-pattern (not this case)
Confirms this is the right moment to re-request

evals.prompt: The reviewer left a comment that I think is just wrong — they're claiming my code doesn't handle a case it actually does handle. The handling is in a different file from the diff. How do I respond?
evals.expected_output: Point to the specific code that handles the case. Not 'no, you're wrong'; instead 'the case is handled by <function/line> in <file>; see the test at <line>'. Make the evidence visible to a reviewer who may not have followed the cross-file call path. If after that the reviewer still disagrees, offer to walk through it on a short call. The case may also indicate the cross-file handling isn't obvious — a comment in the code at the diff site could prevent the next reviewer from being confused too.
assertions: Recommends pointing to the specific code that handles the case
Provides file and line references in the response
References the test as evidence
Suggests a short call as a follow-up if disagreement persists
Considers adding a code comment to prevent future confusion
Treats the reviewer being wrong as a normal occurrence, not a betrayal
Does not respond with 'no, you're wrong' alone

evals.prompt: My reviewer left 4 nits on naming and 1 blocking comment about a potential SQL injection. I want to push back on all 4 nits because I think they're wrong. Should I?
evals.expected_output: Focus the energy on the blocking comment first — the SQL injection concern is the load-bearing item. Address it correctly (likely fix or surface why it's actually safe). For the 4 nits: the cost of arguing 4 nits is high; the cost of doing them is low. Default to doing the nits unless they actively make the code worse, even if you'd prefer the original. The 'ship vs argue' math is in favor of just doing them. If one of the nits genuinely makes the code worse, decline that one specifically with a reason; do the other three.
assertions: Recommends prioritizing the blocking comment (SQL injection) first
Recommends treating nits as low-stakes; do them rather than argue
References the 'ship vs argue' tradeoff
Allows declining a nit only if it actively makes the code worse
Does not recommend arguing all 4 nits
Does not recommend ignoring the blocking comment in favor of the nits
